skill.name: torch-geometric
skill.description: Build and evaluate graph neural network workflows with PyTorch Geometric for node classification, graph classification, link prediction, heterogeneous graphs, and molecular learning.
skill.summary: Use PyTorch Geometric patterns, references, and helper scripts to build graph neural network projects.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but semantic review found they are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, PyTorch model.eval() calls, and machine learning terminology. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malicious command execution, or obfuscated behavior was found. Residual low risk remains because community helper scripts can write user-selected files and examples may download PyG datasets or dependencies.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown code fences and inline package names in documentation. They show install commands and PyG examples, but the skill does not execute them automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are fenced Markdown examples or inline code markers. No interpreter, subprocess call, or shell execution path is present in those locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: PyTorch Evaluation Mode Flagged as Dynamic Execution
low_findings.description: The eval findings are calls to model.eval(), which switches PyTorch modules into evaluation mode. This is not Python eval() and does not execute dynamic input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The calls are method invocations on model objects in training and inference examples. There is no use of Python eval() or user-controlled code evaluation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: ML Terms Flagged as Weak Cryptography or Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings map to graph learning terms such as GCN, GAT, Diffusion, ComplEx, RotatE, and dataset names. These are model or dataset references, not cryptographic or reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is PyTorch Geometric layer, transform, and dataset documentation. I found no evidence that these terms are used for hashing, crypto, host discovery, or network scanning.

low_findings.title: Intended Local File Write in Template Generator
low_findings.description: The template generator writes a generated Python model file to a user-supplied output path. This is expected functionality, but users should avoid running it with untrusted paths or elevated privileges.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is direct and intentional, and the destination comes from a CLI argument. The risk is low because it is a local developer utility and there is no hidden execution or exfiltration path.

low_findings.title: Expected Dataset and Dependency Network Access
low_findings.description: The skill documents installing PyG wheels and loading public datasets. These actions may use network access when a user chooses to run them, but they are normal for a graph machine learning skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network-related behavior is linked to official documentation, wheel installation, or PyG dataset loaders. I found no credential collection, custom exfiltration endpoint, or suspicious hidden request.

content.value_statement: Graph machine learning projects require careful choices about data objects, layers, transforms, and training loops. This skill provides PyTorch Geometric guidance, reference material, and helper scripts for common GNN workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains PyTorch Geometric Data and HeteroData usage for graph learning tasks.
Provides patterns for node classification, graph classification, and link prediction.
Summarizes PyG layers including GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, GIN, and heterogeneous graph layers.
Documents common datasets, transforms, mini-batching, and neighbor sampling workflows.
Includes helper scripts for benchmarking models, visualizing graphs, and generating GNN templates.
limitations: It does not install PyTorch Geometric or resolve CUDA wheel compatibility by itself.
Examples may need adjustment for custom schemas, very large graphs, or production training infrastructure.
Benchmark results depend on hardware, PyTorch versions, dataset splits, and random seeds.
Helper scripts are educational utilities and should be reviewed before use in managed environments.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Graph Neural Network
use_cases.description: Create a first PyG model for Cora, CiteSeer, PubMed, ENZYMES, or similar benchmark datasets.

use_cases.title: Choose Layers and Transforms
use_cases.description: Compare graph convolution, attention, aggregation, and preprocessing options for a specific graph task.

use_cases.title: Review GNN Project Structure
use_cases.description: Use the scripts and references to structure training loops, evaluation, visualization, and dataset handling.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Node Classifier
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a citation network task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me build a PyTorch Geometric node classification workflow for Cora. Explain the Data object, model choice, training loop, and evaluation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Select a GNN Layer
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, and GIN for my graph task. Recommend one layer family and explain the tradeoffs for data size, edge features, and interpretability.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Dataset Preprocessing
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PyTorch Geometric preprocessing plan for my dataset. Include transforms, train and validation splits, batching strategy, and checks for feature normalization.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Advanced GNN Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review for a production-style workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my PyTorch Geometric pipeline for scalability and correctness. Focus on neighbor sampling, heterogeneous graph support, metric design, reproducibility, and failure modes.

output_examples.input: I need a graph classifier for molecular property prediction.
output_examples.output: A recommended workflow with dataset selection, graph batching, candidate GNN layers, pooling strategy, metrics, and validation checks.

output_examples.input: My node classification model is overfitting on Cora.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting plan covering dropout, weight decay, split quality, feature normalization, hidden size, early stopping, and baseline comparisons.

output_examples.input: I want to visualize a PyG Data object before training.
output_examples.output: A visualization approach that limits large graphs, selects a layout, maps node labels or masks to colors, and saves a figure for inspection.

best_practices: Start with a small benchmark dataset before moving to custom graph data.
Validate tensor shapes, masks, class balance, and edge direction before training.
Record PyTorch, CUDA, PyG, dataset split, and random seed details with each experiment.
anti_patterns: Do not choose a complex heterogeneous or attention model before validating a simple baseline.
Do not compare benchmark runs without controlling data splits, seeds, and training budgets.
Do not run helper scripts with unreviewed output paths or elevated permissions.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps users build, explain, and review PyTorch Geometric graph neural network workflows.

faq.question: Does it support both node and graph classification?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes guidance and examples for node classification, graph classification, and link prediction.

faq.question: Can it help with molecular graph learning?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers molecular datasets and graph classification patterns that are common in chemistry workflows.

faq.question: Does it run training automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and helper scripts, but users choose when to run commands or scripts.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention network access?
faq.answer: PyG examples may install packages or download public datasets when users run them.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found low residual risk and no evidence of malicious behavior.
